chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill
chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill

**Written evidence submitted by the Open Rights Group (OSB116)**
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill

**Written evidence submitted by the Open Rights Group (OSB116)**

chunks.header: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill Call for Evidence
chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill Call for Evidence

Open Rights Group (ORG) is the leading UK-based digital campaigning organisation. We work to protect fundamental rights to privacy and free speech online, including data protection, the impacts of the use of data on vulnerable groups, and online surveillance. With over 20,000 active supporters, we are a grassroots organisation with local groups ac ross the UK. We have worked on this Bill throughout the ‘online harms’ processes and consultations, and both Digital Economy Acts (2010 and 2017), accurately highlighting which parts of both DEAs would prove extraordinarily difficult to implement practical ly or fairly.
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety (Re-committed Clauses and Schedules) Bill Call for Evidence

Open Rights Group (ORG) is the leading UK-based digital campaigning organisation. We work to protect fundamental rights to privacy and free speech online, including data protection, the impacts of the use of data on vulnerable groups, and online surveillance. With over 20,000 active supporters, we are a grassroots organisation with local groups ac ross the UK. We have worked on this Bill throughout the ‘online harms’ processes and consultations, and both Digital Economy Acts (2010 and 2017), accurately highlighting which parts of both DEAs would prove extraordinarily difficult to implement practical ly or fairly.

chunks.text:
Section heading: Perspective

It is very concerning to see a shift in emphasis in the Bill, away from a requirement to merely "take down" harmful content, to "acting against users" in order to restrict those communications. Online platforms, social media and messaging apps are now used on a daily basis by tens of millions of people in the UK for personal, business as well as social communications. So much so that their usage has outstripped the conventional wired telephone. These platforms are not only somewhere to express opinions, but to seek information. Nothing precludes the government from seeking to have content removed where the is actual evidence that it does cause harm, as for example, was revealed by the Molly Russell inquest. But the government also has a duty, under human rights law, to safeguard the rights of those millions who are using the service lawfully. Any restriction on this right must be clear and precise, and prescribed by law. As an overall comment, the Bill fails to honour the State’s duty to guarantee those rights. This is our perspective.
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Perspective

It is very concerning to see a shift in emphasis in the Bill, away from a requirement to merely "take down" harmful content, to "acting against users" in order to restrict those communications. Online platforms, social media and messaging apps are now used on a daily basis by tens of millions of people in the UK for personal, business as well as social communications. So much so that their usage has outstripped the conventional wired telephone. These platforms are not only somewhere to express opinions, but to seek information. Nothing precludes the government from seeking to have content removed where the is actual evidence that it does cause harm, as for example, was revealed by the Molly Russell inquest. But the government also has a duty, under human rights law, to safeguard the rights of those millions who are using the service lawfully. Any restriction on this right must be clear and precise, and prescribed by law. As an overall comment, the Bill fails to honour the State’s duty to guarantee those rights. This is our perspective.

chunks.header: Section 1/ Key concerns from a rights viewpoint:
chunks.text:
Section heading: Section 1/ Key concerns from a rights viewpoint:

· It is false to suggest that the changes under scrutiny by the Bill Committee will protect free speech. The removal of Clauses 12 and 13 does not affect the underlying framework of the Bill that establishes rules by which private companies will police restrict content posted by users – that’s you and me. In fact, the new route chosen by the government risks merely switching from a state-mandated rule to a privately-defined one.

· The replacement of Clauses 12 and 13 with New Clauses 2-6 raises new concerns around the way that the State will enforce the terms and conditions of private companies with regard to online speech. They will be able to restrict any content as long as they tell you. They can impose restrictions at their own discretion, based on their own definitions. It risks a fragmented landscape where users will never know what they may or may not post and to which platform any given rule applies.

· Overall, the changes to the Bill will shift the onus of the legislation onto age assurance - so-called "age gating" that will have to be implemented by platforms with user bases that span generations of young and old. It’s a spectre that raises new and grave risks around biometric surveillance of children and the governance of these technologies that will become mandatory.

· The changes to user empowerment in Clause 14, far from empowering anyone, are in fact a closet way for social media platforms working with the UK authorities to decide what content should be seen or hidden. Once again, it will create a fragmented landscape with no transparency where it will be difficult to obtain redress.

· The entire framework created by this law, establishes ways to use technology in order to remove, ban, demote speech and limit access to information. When online platform restrict content, it is not a breach of contract, but a breach of rights.

· We urge the Committee to strengthen Clause 20 enshrining the right to freedom of expression and privacy as it stands in the Human Rights Act.

· In view of the complexity of the technological impact combined with potentially serious and wide-scale interference with rights, we are very concerned that the Bill Committee has very little time to scrutinise these amendments.

chunks.header: Section 2/ Removal of Clauses 12 -13 and replacement with NC2-NC6
chunks.text:
Section heading: Section 2/ Removal of Clauses 12 -13 and replacement with NC2-NC6

The amendments under consideration (NC2- NC6) will create an outcome that switches the full responsibility for any content deemed "legal by harmful" from the government and moves it onto online platforms. It is questionable whether it is removed from the Bill or merely re-drafted to hide the impact.

The amendments state that the Internet services in scope must not "act against their users except in accordance with their Terms and conditions". The Bill anticipates certain forms of restriction on users’ content, such as taking it down, restricting access to it, giving it lower priority ( which includes shadow banning) and banning users. All of these actions are considered as interference with restrictions on freedom of expression, and therefore the rights is engaged by this Bill. The language of "restricting access" is troubling because the implied meaning emphasises a policy of restricting free speech, not protecting it.

The amendments apply to Category 1 Services only, but without knowing the precise definition of a Category 1 service, it is difficult to know the consequences of these amendments. A law that has such an impact on freedom of expression as this Bill has, should be able to say precisely on the face of the Bill, what type of services it will affect and how it will affect them.

The platforms will be able to restrict any content as long as they tell you in their Terms and Conditions. They can impose restrictions at their own discretion.

If it is in their Terms and Conditions, users will have limited redress at best, when a mistake is made. False flags are a common occurrence. What happens if there is a contextual interpretation that does not match the terms of service? A disagreement? Clause 18 establishes a complaints procedure, but not an appeal procedure. Users can complain about breach of contract, but not appeal the interference with their freedom of expression rights. We would urge Parliament to correct this, and introduce an appeal procedure.

**We urge the Bill Committee to reject these amendments on the basis that they hand over power to the online platforms without proper safeguards for freedom of expression.**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Section 3/ Clause 14 "user empowerment"

This is about a filter to be offered by online platforms to users who may optionally choose to use it and thereby exclude certain types of content from their newsfeeds and timelines. New Clause 2 seems to exclude this content from being considered a restriction. However, we would say that a filter is a form of interference with freedom of expression, and would engage that right.

There is an open question as to how the content to be filtered will be selected. The criteria for selection will certainly make a difference for the user experience. It is not clear who will determine what content meets the criteria. It could be that the platforms determine it for themselves. Alternatively, New Clause 1 says that Ofcom will provide guidance to online platforms on the kind of content that should be filtered. Specifically, Ofcom must provide examples of this content. Ofcom must consult with the Secretary of State (Clause 38) who will have powers to modify Ofcom’s Codes of Practice (Clause 41) and its strategic priorities will be set by the Secretary of State (Clause 149).

User empowerment would seem to be filtered content determined by the UK authorities and implemented by private companies, notwithstanding that they should consider freedom of expression when implementing the filters ( Amendment 31). The State will determine the broad criteria for the filters. Ofcom, the regulator will enforce the implementation of the filters. It’s questionable if there will be any transparency. The matter of redress available to users is also questionable.

A filter – even if optional – that is a mandatory requirement of the State is arguably still a restriction of speech. **We call on the Bill Committee to delete this Clause.**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Section 4/ Amendment 3 age assurance

An amendment to Clause 11 (Amendment 3) confirms that the government intends age assurance in the sense of platforms with mixed user base of adults and children: "Age assurance to identify who is a child user or which age group a child user is in". This is not about excluding children from an adults-only platforms but it concerns restricting access by children of content available on a mixed platform. It is also about excluding different age-groups of children. The technology required must therefore be good enough to identify not only if someone is a child, but what age of child they are. As we understand it, the only way to do this is to take biometric scans of all users. Biometric scans could mean for example, scanning head or hand measurements, or voices. The risks here of collecting vast pools of biometric data are considerable. We believe it would be irresponsible for Parliament to put this into law, without proper governance in place for the systems and industries that will be operating those systems, and without clear specifications as to how these systems will operate.

The language in Clause 11 of "prevent encountering" is deeply concerning. This amendment means restricting access on the basis of age. Children also have a right to freedom of expression, which includes the right to access information.

An alternative method would entail an "upload filter". This is an automated system that intercepts content on upload and sweeps it away before the user can publish. Our concern here is not with the policy aim to tackle criminal content related to child sexual abuse, but with the possibility of a large number of false positives and likely suppression of lawful content. We reiterate that any restriction must be spelled out clearly and precisely on the face of the Bill. This amendment requires urgent and detailed attention.

The whole question of age assurance raises deep questions around rights for children and adults via the introduction of age assurance systems that are powered by artificial intelligence and algorithms. This Bill is bringing them in via the back door. These questions should be investigated and understood before Parliament can be asked to approve these measures. **On that basis, we urge the committee to reject this amendment and to remove the requirement for age assurance.**

chunks.header: Section 5/ Clause 20 Freedom of Expression and Restricting Access
chunks.text:
Section heading: Section 5/ Clause 20 Freedom of Expression and Restricting Access

This is a law that is predicated on the restriction of speech rights, as outlined above. The entire framework created by this law, establishes ways to use technology in order to remove, ban, demote speech and limit access to information. Parliament has a responsibility to protect human rights, and especially, that most precious of rights, freedom of expression. The amendment to Clause 20 amends "have regard to freedom of expression within the law" to "have particular regard to". It still leaves a weak protection for free speech in this Bill.

**We urge the Committee to enshrine the right to freedom of expression as it stands in the Human Rights Right, with Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights.** We note that there is little appetite on either side of the House, or in civil society, for the government’s plans to scrap the Human Rights Act. We suggest that it provides a pragmatic balance between the free speech rights of the individual and the imposition of restrictions by the State.

The encapsulation of a restriction as merely on someone seeing content, or banning someone, is too simplistic, and needs revision. Freedom of expression is not only a right to speak – impart information – but also a right to access information. This right to access is important for the Bill which will require search engines – who facilitate access to information – to down-rank content – which reduces its visibility. If lawful content is down-ranked, it represents is potentially a restriction of rights.

Furthermore, the Bill ignores the possibility that content published by users could be demoted or subjected to limited distribution by recommender systems in people’s newsfeeds and timelines. These include Facebook News Feed and similar algorithmically-driven distribution systems on other platforms. Demoting or limiting distribution so that for example, means that your posts will not be seen in news feeds. This is a form of restriction of speech.

**We call on the Committee to insert an amendment that offers users an effective appeal process.** The redress available to users who have been wrongfully or incorrectly restricted is limited. If content is taken down in error, it is vital that users have a means of redress. For example, algorithmic systems are context-blind. They cannot tell the difference between a quote used as a political comment, and a quote used in its literal meaning. See for example, this case examined by the Facebook Oversight Board.

They cannot tell the difference between a quote used as a political comment, and a quote used in its literal meaning. See for example, this case examined by the Facebook Oversight Board.

chunks.header: Section 6/ Clause 20 and Clause 203 Interference with Privacy
chunks.text:
Section heading: Section 6/ Clause 20 and Clause 203 Interference with Privacy

The review of Clause 20 also opens up the question of privacy rights. Whilst not subject to a specific amendment, the opening up of these Clauses presents an opportunity to review and rectify one the serious breach of privacy rights created by the Bill. We trust the Committee will allow us the opportunity to expand on this point.

**We urge the Committee to enshrine the right to privacy as it stands in the Human Rights Right, with Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights.**

interference with privacy is not merely a breach of contract, but is a breach of a right. In Clause 203, also under review the Bill defines content as " communicated publicly or privately". It is stated in the government’s Impact Assessment that the word "privately" brings encrypted private messaging services into scope. This is a dangerous move because if the measures in the Bill that specifically refer to private communications (Clause 106 the "spy clause") are ever implemented, the result would be widespread surveillance of that daily chat of over 40 million UK users. We have outlined how this is the effect of the Bill in this briefing. The implementation would entail the use of automated content moderation systems on private chats. We note that the government says it will be doing this, however, our analysis of the Bill indicates that that the drafters of this Bill did understand very well that they were mandating content moderation on private end-to-end encrypted messaging services.

*December 2022*
